She mentions that money was tight, and time was limited. She had to tell her kids that money doesn't grow on trees. Then, after all her kids left home, on Mother's Day, Marvin planted a money tree with $100 and a note saying he owed eight hours of digging. Marvin kept his promise by digging a garden bed, and the other kids bought her gardening tools and stuff. Now she has a beautiful garden and feels her children are with her when she tends her flowers. The story ends with her saying she's still not sure money grows on trees, but she knows love does. Now, let's look at the options: A) She is a bad-tempered woman. Hmm. The story doesn't mention her being bad-tempered. B) She believes money can grow on a tree now. Wait, at the end, she says, "I'm still not sure that money grows on trees. But I know love does!" So she's not believing that money grows on trees. So B is incorrect. The opposite is true. C) She is a writer. The story is written in first person, but there's no mention of her being a writer. The story is about her family and the garden. The fact that she had to manage everything for her kids and later worked on her garden suggests she's hard-working in housekeeping.
